content.text: Decentralized Identity

Decentralized Identity is a standards-based approach to digital identity where individuals own and control their credentials — without depending on a central authority (like a social login provider or government database) to vouch for them. Microsoft Entra Verified ID is Microsoft's implementation of this model, built on the W3C Decentralized Identifiers (DID) and Verifiable Credentials (VC) standards.

attrs.alt: Decentralized Identity trust triangle showing Issuer, Holder, and Verifier roles connected through a DID Registry

content.text: Core Concepts
Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs)

A DID is a globally unique identifier that the subject controls — not a third party. Unlike usernames or email addresses, a DID is cryptographically generated and anchored to a DID registry (blockchain, DNS, or other verifiable data registry). The DID resolves to a DID Document containing the public keys and service endpoints for the DID subject.
Example DID: did:web:verifiedid.contoso.com. This resolves to a JSON DID Document hosted at https://verifiedid.contoso.com/.well-known/did.json, containing the public key used to sign credentials.
Verifiable Credentials (VCs)

A Verifiable Credential is a tamper-proof digital credential issued by a trusted party (the Issuer) to a subject (the Holder). The VC contains claims about the subject (e.g., 'John Smith has a verified employment status at Contoso') and is cryptographically signed by the issuer's DID key so any verifier can check its authenticity without calling back to the issuer.
Verifiable Presentations (VPs)

When a Holder wants to prove something to a Verifier, they create a Verifiable Presentation — a package containing one or more VCs (or selective disclosures of claims from those VCs), signed with the Holder's own DID key. The VP proves both that the Holder possesses the VC and that it was not tampered with since issuance.
Microsoft Entra Verified ID Architecture

Microsoft Entra Verified ID implements the W3C VC standard and adds enterprise-grade lifecycle management, audit trails, and integration with Microsoft Authenticator as the credential wallet. Organizations can become issuers, verifiers, or both.
Component
Role
Technology
Entra Verified ID Service
Issues and verifies credentials on behalf of the tenant
REST API, hosted by Microsoft
Credential manifest (rules + display)
Defines claim schema, data sources, and UI presentation
JSON hosted in Azure Blob or your CDN
Microsoft Authenticator
End-user credential wallet (stores and presents VCs)
Mobile app (iOS/Android)
DID Registry (did:web)
Anchors the issuer's public DID document
HTTPS-hosted JSON at /.well-known/did.json
Request Service API
Initiates issuance and presentation request flows
REST API — requires Azure subscription
Entra Verified ID portal
Self-service setup for credential types and verification logic
Azure portal blade
Setting Up Entra Verified ID (PowerShell + REST)
Enable and Configure the Service

# Step 1: Register the Entra Verified ID service principal in your tenant
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Application.ReadWrite.All', 'DelegatedPermissionGrant.ReadWrite.All'

# The Verifiable Credentials service has a well-known app ID
$vcServiceAppId = '3db474b9-6a0c-4840-96ac-1fceb342124f'
$sp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '$vcServiceAppId'" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
if (-not $sp) {
    $sp = New-MgServicePrincipal -AppId $vcServiceAppId
    Write-Host 'Verifiable Credentials service principal created.'
} else {
    Write-Host 'Verifiable Credentials service principal already exists.'
}

# Step 2: Grant the service principal admin consent for the required permissions
# (This is done in the Azure portal under Entra Verified ID > Setup)
# Navigate to: https://entra.microsoft.com/#blade/Microsoft_AAD_DecentralizedIdentity/InitialMenuBlade
Write-Host 'Complete setup in the Entra Verified ID portal.'
Create a Credential Type (Rules + Display JSON)
Credential Issuance Flow (Request Service API)

The issuance flow is triggered by your web application calling the Entra Verified ID Request Service API. The API returns a QR code or deep link that the user scans with Microsoft Authenticator. The Authenticator fetches the credential manifest, collects any required claims, and presents them to the VC service, which signs and returns the credential to the wallet.
User visits your issuance page (e.g., 'Get your Employee Badge credential').
Your web app calls the Request Service API /createIssuanceRequest endpoint.
The API returns a QR code URL and a request token.
User scans the QR code with Microsoft Authenticator.
Authenticator fetches the credential manifest and prompts the user.
VC service signs the credential and delivers it to the Authenticator wallet.
User's wallet now holds the Verifiable Credential.
Issuance Request (Python)

import requests, json

TENANT_ID      = '<your-tenant-id>'
CLIENT_ID      = '<your-app-client-id>'
CLIENT_SECRET  = '<your-app-client-secret>'
AUTHORITY      = f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}'
VC_SERVICE_SCOPE = '3db474b9-6a0c-4840-96ac-1fceb342124f/.default'

# Get access token for VC service
token_resp = requests.post(
    f'{AUTHORITY}/oauth2/v2.0/token',
    data={
        'grant_type':    'client_credentials',
        'client_id':     CLIENT_ID,
        'client_secret': CLIENT_SECRET,
        'scope':         VC_SERVICE_SCOPE,
    }
)
access_token = token_resp.json()['access_token']

# Create issuance request
issuance_payload = {
    'includeQRCode': True,
    'callback': {
        'url':   'https://app.contoso.com/api/vc/issuance-callback',
        'state': 'unique-session-state-12345',
        'headers': { 'api-key': '<your-callback-api-key>' }
    },
    'authority': 'did:web:verifiedid.contoso.com',
    'registration': { 'clientName': 'Contoso Employee Portal' },
    'type': 'VerifiedEmployee',
    'manifest': 'https://verifiedid.did.msidentity.com/v1.0/<tenant-id>/verifiableCredential/contracts/VerifiedEmployee',
    'claims': {
        'displayName': 'John Smith',
        'jobTitle':    'Software Engineer',
        'department':  'Engineering'
    }
}

response = requests.post(
    f'https://verifiedid.did.msidentity.com/v1.0/<tenant-id>/verifiableCredentials/createIssuanceRequest',
    headers={ 'Authorization': f'Bearer {access_token}', 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    json=issuance_payload
)

result = response.json()
print('QR Code URL:', result.get('qrCode'))
print('Request ID:', result.get('requestId'))
Credential Verification Flow

When a Relying Party (Verifier) needs to confirm a user's identity or qualification, it initiates a presentation request. The user's Authenticator wallet is prompted to share the relevant credential, and the VC service validates the cryptographic proof and returns the claims to the verifier.
Verification Request (Python)
DID Methods Supported in Entra Verified ID
DID Method
Anchoring
Best For
Notes
did:web
HTTPS + DNS (your domain)
Enterprise issuers with a verified domain
DID document hosted at /.well-known/did.json; simplest to set up
did:ion
Bitcoin blockchain (ION Layer 2)
High-assurance anchoring without a central authority
Immutable; no domain dependency; slower resolution; deprecated for new tenants
did:jwk
In-memory key material only
Testing and ephemeral identities
Not persistent; not recommended for production
Microsoft Entra Verified ID now defaults to did:web for new tenants. did:ion (ION network) is supported for existing tenants but new issuers should use did:web with a verified domain.
Revoking a Verifiable Credential

Entra Verified ID supports credential revocation via a status list embedded in the credential. When you revoke a credential, the status list is updated and verifiers checking the credential's validity will see it as revoked. Revocation does not notify the holder's wallet.

# Revoke a specific issued credential by its contract and credential ID
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'VerifiableCredential.ReadWrite.All'

$tenantId     = '<your-tenant-id>'
$contractId   = 'VerifiedEmployee'
$credentialId = '<credential-id-to-revoke>'   # from issuance callback

# Call the Entra Verified ID admin API to revoke
Invoke-MgGraphRequest `
    -Method POST `
    -Uri "https://verifiedid.did.msidentity.com/v1.0/$tenantId/verifiableCredentials/contracts/$contractId/revoke" `
    -Body (@{ id = $credentialId } | ConvertTo-Json) `
    -ContentType 'application/json'

Write-Host "Credential $credentialId revoked successfully."
Issuance Callback Handling

The Entra Verified ID Request Service delivers real-time status updates to your application via a callback URL. Your callback endpoint must handle events for both the issuance and presentation flows. Callbacks are POST requests with a JSON body signed with a callback API key you specified in the request.
Issuance Callback Payload
Presentation Callback Payload
Monitoring with KQL

Entra Verified ID operations are audited in the Azure Active Directory Audit Logs. You can query these logs in Log Analytics to track issuance, presentation, and revocation events for compliance and operations monitoring.
Audit VC Issuance Events

// Track Verifiable Credential issuance and presentation events
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where Category == 'VerifiedId'
| extend Operation   = OperationName
| extend CredType    = tostring(AdditionalDetails[0].value)
| extend RequestedBy = InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName
| summarize
    Count     = count(),
    FirstSeen = min(TimeGenerated),
    LastSeen  = max(TimeGenerated)
    by Operation, CredType
| order by Count desc
Daily Issuance Volume

// Chart daily VC issuance volume for capacity planning
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(90d)
| where Category == 'VerifiedId'
| where OperationName == 'Issue VerifiedId'
| summarize IssuanceCount = count() by bin(TimeGenerated, 1d)
| order by TimeGenerated asc
Selective Disclosure and Privacy Considerations

One of the key privacy benefits of Verifiable Credentials is selective disclosure. Instead of presenting an entire credential, the holder can share only the specific claims needed for a transaction. Entra Verified ID supports selective disclosure through the presentation request configuration.

Privacy principle: the holder presents only what is necessary. A bar should need only 'over 18: true' — not the holder's full date of birth or government ID number. VCs enable this minimum-disclosure pattern by design.
Configure the presentation request to request specific claims from the credential using the 'claims' filter in 'requestedCredentials'.
The VC service validates the claim against the full credential but only returns the requested claims to your callback.
For high-privacy scenarios, consider using JSON-LD frames or BBS+ signatures (which enable selective disclosure at the cryptographic level without re-issuance).

Entra Verified ID's current implementation (JsonWebSignature2020) shares all claims in the credential; selective disclosure is enforced at the application layer by filtering what you store from the callback.
Never log the full verifiedCredentialsData payload to persistent storage — extract only the claims your application requires and discard the rest.
Common Errors and Troubleshooting
Error
Cause
Resolution
AADSTS700016 on VC API call
App registration does not have access to the VC service scope
Grant 3db474b9-6a0c-4840-96ac-1fceb342124f/.default admin consent
DID document not found
did:web domain not configured or /.well-known/did.json returns 404
Ensure the domain is verified in Entra Verified ID setup and the .well-known file is publicly accessible
Callback not received
Firewall blocks inbound POST from Microsoft to your callback URL
Whitelist Microsoft IP ranges for Azure outbound; ensure callback endpoint is publicly reachable (no localhost)
Credential not appearing in Authenticator
Deep link or QR code expired (default 5-minute TTL)
Re-generate the issuance request; do not reuse old QR codes
Presentation request rejected by wallet
Requested issuer DID not in acceptedIssuers list, or credential is revoked
Verify the issuer DID matches exactly; check revocation status via the admin API
'issuance_error' callback with error 'claimsRetrievalFailed'
idToken hint claims missing required fields from manifest schema
Ensure all required claims (as defined in rules.json) are passed in the 'claims' payload
Summary

Decentralized Identity with Microsoft Entra Verified ID gives organizations a standards-based, privacy-respecting way to issue and verify digital credentials without requiring a centralized identity registry.
DIDs are cryptographically controlled identifiers anchored in a verifiable registry (did:web uses HTTPS/DNS).
Verifiable Credentials carry tamper-proof, issuer-signed claims about the holder's identity or qualifications.
The trust triangle (Issuer → Holder → Verifier) routes credentials through the holder's wallet rather than via the issuer — preserving holder privacy.
Microsoft Authenticator is the primary VC wallet; credentials are issued and presented via QR code deep links.
The Entra Verified ID Request Service API exposes REST endpoints for issuance and presentation requests.
Credentials support expiry and revocation via status lists; verifiers can be configured to reject revoked or expired credentials.
did:web is the recommended DID method for enterprise tenants; did:ion is legacy.
